---
title: "Joint visual + eDNA modelling for marine megafauna"
subtitle: "Project goals and model overview"
author: "MMARINeDNA / eDNAVisualJointModel"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    embed-resources: true
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    progress: true
    history: false
    chalkboard: false
    fig-align: center
    fig-cap-location: bottom
    code-fold: true
    code-line-numbers: false
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo      = FALSE,
  message   = FALSE,
  warning   = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center",
  dpi       = 110
)
```

## Why joint modelling?

For the same species, in the same area, we have **two complementary survey streams**:

::: {.fragment}
- **Line-transect (LT)** — visual sightings from ship-board observers along systematic transects. Decades of methodology, well-calibrated detection theory, but expensive and sparse.
:::

::: {.fragment}
- **Environmental DNA (eDNA)** — water samples assayed by species-specific qPCR + multi-species metabarcoding (MARVER1). Cheap, high coverage, but the link from "reads in a bottle" to "animals in the water" is indirect.
:::

::: {.fragment}
The two methods see the same animals through different observation processes. **Fitting them jointly** lets each correct the other and produces a single defensible density estimate.
:::

## State-space view of the joint model

A **single latent density surface** generates *both* observation streams.

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-align: center
flowchart TB
  L["<b>Latent density surface</b><br/>log λ<sub>s</sub>(x, y, z) = μ<sub>s</sub> + f<sub>s</sub>(x, y, z)<br/>f<sub>s</sub> ∼ GP(0, K)"]
  L --> E["<b>eDNA observation model</b><br/>qPCR Ct values<br/>+ MARVER1 read counts"]
  L --> V["<b>Visual observation model</b><br/>line-transect sightings<br/>+ group sizes"]

  classDef latent fill:#fff5d6,stroke:#b08000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#222;
  classDef obs    fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#3a6ea5,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#222;
  class L latent;
  class E,V obs;
```

The shared latent field is what ties the two data streams together. Posterior inference borrows strength across methods.

## Latent density: GP submodel

For each species $s$, log-density at location $\mathbf{x} = (x, y, z)$ is

$$
\log \lambda_s(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \mu_s \;+\; f_s(\mathbf{x}),
\qquad
f_s \sim \mathcal{GP}\!\bigl(0,\, K(\ell_x, \ell_y, \ell_z)\bigr).
$$

::: {.fragment}
- 3-D anisotropic squared-exponential kernel — separate length-scales in cross-shore $x$, alongshore $y$, and depth $z$.
- Fit via the **Hilbert-space GP approximation** [Riutort-Mayol et al. 2023] — replaces the dense $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ GP with a basis expansion of size $M \ll N$.
- All species share the same kernel structure but fit independent length-scales.
:::

## eDNA observation submodel

Each water sample $i$ has an expected per-litre target copy concentration

$$
\mathbb{E}[\text{copies}_{i,s}] \;=\; c_s \cdot \lambda_s(\mathbf{x}_i) \cdot \zeta(\mathbf{x}_i) \cdot v_i,
$$

where $c_s$ is the species-specific shedding / recovery factor, $\zeta$ is the depth-of-sample multiplier, and $v_i$ is volume filtered.

::: {.fragment}
- **qPCR**: hurdle on positive Ct values via a fitted standard curve (species-specific primers — only the target species).
- **Metabarcoding (MARVER1)**: zero-inflated Beta-Binomial across the three target species, with a "junk" category for non-targets. $\phi$ parameterised on log total copies.
:::

## Line-transect observation submodel

Each transect segment $j$ yields a Poisson number of *groups* with rate

$$
n_j \;\sim\; \text{Poisson}\!\bigl(\, 2 L_j \cdot \mathrm{ESW}_s \cdot \lambda_s^{(g)}(\mathbf{x}_j) \,\bigr),
$$

where $L_j$ is segment length, $\mathrm{ESW}_s = \int_0^\infty g_s(d)\,\mathrm{d}d$ is the effective strip half-width, and $\lambda_s^{(g)} = \lambda_s / \bar{m}_s$ is animal density divided by mean group size.

::: {.fragment}
- $g_s(d)$ is a half-normal detection function; PWSD's $\sigma$ depends on group size (larger groups easier to spot).
- **Jensen correction** on $\mathrm{ESW}$ + size-bias correction in the group-size sub-model so that observed groups are unbiased estimates of population-level group sizes.
- Group sizes drawn from species-specific distributions (NB for humpback, log-normal for PWSD).
:::

## Per-species data: humpback whale

![Line-transect (left) + MARVER1 eDNA (right) observations for humpback whale, 2018 CCES survey + MURI sampling.](../figures/lt_edna_humpback.png){width=100%}

::: {.fragment}
376 LT sightings (mostly group size 1–2) + 36 eDNA detections, both clustered nearshore. Strong spatial concordance.
:::

## Per-species data: Pacific white-sided dolphin

![Line-transect (left) + MARVER1 eDNA (right) observations for Pacific white-sided dolphin.](../figures/lt_edna_pwsd.png){width=100%}

::: {.fragment}
63 LT sightings (group size 2–452) + 60 eDNA detections, both concentrated offshore at the same latitudes. Tighter spatial structure than humpback because PWSD is more habitat-restricted.
:::

## Where this gets us

::: {.fragment}
- **Density estimates** for each species that propagate uncertainty from both observation processes.
:::

::: {.fragment}
- **Calibration** of the eDNA → animals link from the well-understood LT side, and conversely **smooth coverage** from eDNA filling in gaps the transects don't reach.
:::

::: {.fragment}
- A **shared latent field** so when we extrapolate to unsampled cells we are using all available information, not the survey method that happened to visit nearest.
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Status**: simulation pipelines (v1 → v4.1) and a stand-alone distance-sampling pipeline are in place; the joint Stan model is the next integration step.
:::

## References

Riutort-Mayol, G., Bürkner, P.-C., Andersen, M.R., Solin, A., & Vehtari, A. (2023). *Practical Hilbert space approximate Bayesian Gaussian processes for probabilistic programming.* Statistics and Computing 33, 17.

Buckland, S.T. et al. (2001, 2015). *Distance Sampling: methods and applications* / *Introduction to Distance Sampling.*

Project repo: <https://github.com/MMARINeDNA/eDNAVisualJointModel>

Companion notebooks: `notebooks/v3_notebook.html`, `notebooks/v3.2_notebook.html`, `notebooks/distance_v4.1_notebook.html`.
